text: The surgeon begins the procedure with an anterograde salpingectomy using a 5mm ligation sealing device. The round ligament is cut at the medial aspect, followed by dissection towards the infundibulopelvic ligament. The utero-ovarian ligament and adnexal pedicle are severed. Dissection continues on the posterior and anterior leaves of the left broad ligament to circumscribe the left hematocorpus.

The surgeon removes the fallopian tube to improve visibility of the large left hematocorpus, measuring ten centimeters in diameter. The surgical field reveals the internal iliac artery bifurcation and the origin of the uterine artery, which is cut to reduce perioperative bleeding. The dissection plane is deep, with large vessels surrounding the obstructed vagina. The superficial uterine vein is controlled using a ligature sealing device, aiming for a bloodless technique to maintain clear visibility of the dissecting plane.

The surgeon continues dissection toward the left uterosacral ligament to circumscribe the posterior aspect of the large hematocorpus. The vesicovaginal dissection is performed, pulling the bladder downward. The remnant part of the left uterosacral ligament is controlled, with no risk to the ureter due to kidney agenesis. The surgeon proceeds posteriorly to visualize the lower part of the hematocorpus, controlling the artery and vein near it. The uterine artery is controlled at the isthmic uterine area, and the left bladder pillar is cut above the hematocorpus.

The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the paravesical fossa and controlling the superior vesical vein and artery. The peak hematocorpus becomes progressively visible. The vesicovaginal dissection is extended downward to circumscribe the hematocorpus. The uterine corpus is removed, providing a clearer view of the residual cervix and obstructed left hemivagina. The supracervical hysterectomy is performed.

The surgeon uses Podzi forceps to tract the remnant cervix and opens it with a monopolar hook, reaching the obstructed left hemivagina. The fibrotic tissue of the hematocorpus is opened, revealing a large amount of hemociderin clot, which is removed to rinse the obstructed hemivagina. The surgeon uses the ligature sealing device for hemostasis and visibility, while respecting the right functional vagina. The left vagina is marsupialized using the monopolar hook, leaving the remnant vagina open.

The surgeon places all clots inside an endobag and removes them through the left iliac operative trocar. The anterior aspect of the obstructed vagina is excised, followed by the left supracervical hysterectomy using a 15mm morcellator. The final surgical view reveals the right ureter, right hemiuterus, and right hemivagina, while the left hemivagina remains open in the pelvis. An ovariopexy is performed to reduce the risk of ovarian torsion.
